Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd try to sit down and study but I learn best by doing things with my hands, and a lot of the course material was just reading and watching videos which made it hard to stay focused. I'd usually end up studying in bursts when I could feel the panic setting in about deadlines. With 17 courses to juggle, I never really found a consistent rhythm—I was just putting out fires constantly.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and what crisis was happening in my life that week. Some weeks I wouldn't touch anything because I was dealing with work or family stuff, then I'd cram everything into a few desperate nights. I tried to set regular times at the beginning of the semester, but that fell apart pretty quickly when reality hit. Deadlines definitely drove when I studied more than any planned schedule.
Q3: Usually panic about an upcoming deadline would get me to log in. Sometimes I'd get a notification from a peer or see something in our group chat that reminded me I was falling behind. Occasionally I'd log in just to check if I'd missed something important, which honestly just made my anxiety worse most of the time. The peer support program helped because my study buddy would sometimes text me to check in, and that would prompt me to at least look at what needed to be done.
Q4: There were maybe a few weeks where I got into a groove of checking the LMS every couple of days, usually when things felt more manageable. But it never really became automatic for me—something would always come up to break the habit. I think the peer support helped a bit because having someone else counting on me made me feel more accountable. But honestly, routine and I don't get along well, especially when I'm stressed.
Q5: The discussion forums were actually helpful when I used them because I could interact with other students and that felt more active than just reading. The videos were okay but I had to really force myself to pay attention—I'd often do something with my hands while watching to stay engaged. Anything that let me practice or apply concepts felt more useful than passive content. My peer support partner helped me understand things better than any resource on the platform.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the supplementary readings because I just didn't have time and they felt overwhelming. The longer video lectures would sit unwatched because I knew I'd zone out anyway. Some of the optional quizzes I skipped because I was already behind on required work and couldn't handle adding more to my plate. When you're drowning, you don't stop to read the instruction manual, you know?
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see an immediate connection to passing the course or understanding something I was confused about. If it was hands-on or interactive, I was more likely to engage with it properly. When my peers recommended something or we worked through it together, that made it feel more valuable. Activities that felt like busy work or weren't clearly connected to assessments got pushed to the bottom of my priority list.
Q8: Required activities felt like survival—I had to do them or fail even worse than I already was. Optional ones felt like luxuries I couldn't afford when I was already struggling to keep up with the basics. There was always this guilt about not doing the optional stuff because I knew it might help, but my anxiety would spike when I thought about adding more to my workload. Sometimes the optional activities actually seemed more interesting, which made it frustrating that I couldn't get to them.
Q9: Yeah, I downloaded a lot of materials to study offline because my internet isn't always reliable and I couldn't always afford data. I'd print things out at the library when I could and mark them up by hand—that helped me learn better than staring at a screen. I also used YouTube videos and free resources I found online when the course materials weren't clicking for me. My peer support partner and I would meet in person sometimes to work through problems together.
Q10: A lot of my actual learning happened in study sessions with my peer partner that weren't tracked anywhere. I'd spend hours working through printed materials or notes I'd made, and none of that showed up in the system. Sometimes I'd watch a video once online then replay the downloaded version multiple times—the LMS only saw one view. The conversations I had with classmates about course content were some of my most valuable learning moments but they were invisible.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download PDFs and videos when I had good internet access, then study them later at home or on the bus. Sometimes I'd access something once just to save it, knowing I'd need to review it multiple times offline. My limited resources meant I had to be strategic about when I used data versus when I studied from saved materials. The LMS probably thought I barely looked at things when really I was reviewing them repeatedly offline.
Q12: There were several points where everything just felt impossible. When multiple deadlines piled up across my 17 courses, I'd freeze and not know where to start. My anxiety would get so bad that I'd avoid logging in because seeing how behind I was made me feel worse. Personal stuff—money problems, family obligations—would pull my attention away and I'd lose momentum completely. Once I fell behind, catching up felt insurmountable.
Q13: Absolutely, all of those things affected me constantly. The workload was genuinely unmanageable with so many courses, and confusion about material would make me avoid it rather than ask for help. Fatigue was real—I'd be too exhausted to focus even when I had time. Working to support myself meant I often had to choose between studying and making rent, and rent usually won. The LMS became something I dreaded opening because it just showed me how much I was failing.
Q14: So many times. I'd open videos and let them play while my mind wandered because I was too anxious or tired to actually absorb anything. Sometimes I'd scroll through materials just to feel like I was doing something, even though nothing was sticking. There were nights I'd stare at readings for an hour and realize I hadn't processed a single paragraph. The stress made it hard to actually engage even when I was technically present.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The anxiety around them was so intense that avoidance felt like the only way to cope in the moment. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but tomorrow I'd feel worse because now I was even more behind. Some tasks felt so overwhelming that starting them seemed impossible, so I just... didn't. It's a terrible cycle but I didn't know how to break it.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. I'd ignore things until they were urgent, then scramble at the last minute in a panic. The LMS would probably show these bursts of activity right before due dates because that's when desperation overcame my avoidance. Sometimes I'd miss deadlines entirely because by the time I faced them, it was already too late. The pressure helped me focus but also destroyed my mental health.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it could go either way. Positive feedback gave me a temporary boost and made me want to try harder. Negative feedback often sent me spiraling and made me avoid that course even more. The problem was feedback often came too late to help me improve on the next thing. My peer support partner's feedback was more immediate and felt less judgmental, which helped more than official course feedback.
Q18: The structure honestly overwhelmed me. Having 17 courses meant the combined structure was just chaos—different deadlines, different formats, different expectations. When individual courses had clear, simple structures, I did better, but most felt complicated and hard to navigate. The peer support intervention helped because it gave me someone to make sense of the structure with. Without that human element, the course structure just felt like a maze I kept getting lost in.
Q19: Courses with flexible deadlines or multiple small assignments were easier to engage with than ones with big high-stakes exams. When materials were clearly organized and I could see exactly what I needed to do, that reduced my anxiety. But courses with lots of prerequisites or content that built on itself were harder because once I fell behind, I couldn't catch up. The lack of hands-on activities made it harder for me as a kinesthetic learner to stay engaged.
Q20: I think that was literally me. I'd log in, look at things, download stuff, participate in discussions, but then not submit the actual assignments because I couldn't get them done well enough or on time. High activity doesn't mean productive activity—sometimes it's just anxious browsing or trying to figure out what you're supposed to do. You can spend hours in the LMS being lost and stressed without actually completing anything meaningful.
Q21: That's not really my experience since I had low activity AND poor performance. But I can see how someone with good external resources or strong prior knowledge might not need the LMS much. If you're learning effectively through other means—textbooks, study groups, tutoring—the LMS is just for submitting work. Some people are just more efficient learners than me and don't need to spend hours online to understand material.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you log in and what you click on, but they miss so much context. They can't tell if you're actually understanding material or just staring at it blankly. They miss all the offline studying, the peer conversations, the mental health struggles that affect everything. They show behavior but not the reasons behind it or the learning that happens outside the platform. It's like judging a book by counting how many times someone opened it.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with what you're learning, not just going through the motions. It's when you're curious and want to understand, not just pass. For me, engagement happens when I can do something active with the material and when I feel supported rather than overwhelmed. Real engagement is hard to achieve when you're drowning in anxiety and survival mode—it requires mental space I often didn't have.
Q24: The LMS can't show how hard I was actually trying despite my poor results. It can't capture the anxiety attacks before logging in or the tears of frustration when I couldn't understand something. It misses the growth I experienced through peer support and the genuine learning that happened in conversations outside the platform. My circumstances—the financial stress, the impossible course load, the mental health struggles—none of that shows up in the data. I'm not lazy or disengaged; I'm overwhelmed and under-resourced.
